NATO’s projected defense spending for 2026 is set to reach $1.81 trillion, up 11% from the previous year. The US remains the largest spender at $1.03 trillion, while Turkey is expected to spend $48 billion, equivalent to 2.85% of GDP. In terms of defense spending as a share of GDP, Lithuania (5.33%) ranks first, followed by Estonia, Latvia, Poland, Greece, Denmark, and Sweden.

RT by @lookner: A reminder of what the US waiver revocation on Iranian oil sales means -- and doesn't mean: Iran can still sell its oil if it finds a willing buyers (and China has been in the past, and will be again). Yes, Iran will need to discount its oil, but that would be mostly offset by the rise in oil prices triggered by the fresh hostilities. The biggest problem for Iran? It would have to use (again) non-US dollar settlement. Put it all together, and Tehran would be able to sell the ~60 million barrels it has put on the water so far since the blockade was lifted, at relatively good prices (~$65-70 a barrel), making probably ~$4 billion. Only a return to the US Navy blockade would stop Iran from selling its oil. But that means a new war.

RT by @no_itsmyturn: If the Trump administration is serious about restoring pressure on Iran, it should do more than just strike at military targets. The U.S. should sanction the banks that enable Iran — not just the front companies they serve. During Economic Fury, Treasury warned regulators in China, Hong Kong, Oman, and the UAE, and even sent warning letters to two Chinese banks. But those warnings were never followed by sanctions. The first target should be China’s Bank of Kunlun. Treasury confirmed as recently as August 2025 that Bank of Kunlun remains a key piece to moving funds from Beijing to Tehran. In 2012, the U.S. cut Bank of Kunlun off from correspondent account access, but that was largely symbolic. The bank had little exposure to the U.S. financial system. This time should be different. Treasury should designate Bank of Kunlun under EO 13902 and make clear to its parent, China National Petroleum Corporation (CNPC is the one of the world’s largest oil companies), that continuing to operate the bank carries real risk. That’s what Maximum Pressure should look like.

RT by @no_itsmyturn: The administration says negotiations with Iran continue, but the MOU is breaking down. Iran committed to de-escalation and open transit, yet tankers are again being struck near the Strait of Hormuz. Meanwhile, Washington offered sanctions relief and is now clawing it back. So, where does that leave us? The MOU clearly stipulates that the U.S. will not impose any new sanctions on Iran. The closest we've come to that is when the Terrorist Financing Targeting Center (TFTC) (which includes the U.S., Bahrain, Kuwait, Oman, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE) moved last week to jointly target major Hezbollah institutions Al-Qard Al-Hassan and Bayt al-Mal, as well as their senior leaders, though these entities had been targeted by the U.S. long ago. So, will the U.S. apply any sanctions directly on Iran, thereby signaling the MOU is effectively dead? And if that happens, then what?

Trump’ın yaptırımlar ile ilgili sözleri sonrası yazdığım iki postta anlattıklarımı şu şekilde bırakayım: Birçok kişinin gözden kaçırdığı önemli bir nokta var gibi: CAATSA ve F-35 meselesi birbirini etkileyen ama tam bağlayan şeyler değil gibi. Trump, yasal şartların karşılanması halinde CAATSA yaptırımlarını kaldırabilir. Kongreye bildirim ile bunu yapabilir. Ancak bu, Türkiye’nin otomatik olarak F-35 programına geri döneceği anlamına gelmez. Çünkü Kongre, Türkiye S-400 sistemine sahip olduğu sürece F-35 transferlerini ayrı bir düzenlemeyle yasakladı. Başka bir ifadeyle, CAATSA’nın kaldırılması F-35 onayı anlamına gelmiyor. Bağlayıcı nitelikteki hukuki engelin somut koşulu ortadan kalkmamış oluyor başka bir tabirle. Mealen hep bildiğimiz şey: Türkiye S-400’lere sahip olduğu sürece, F-35 transferlerinin önündeki hukuki engel devam ediyor. S-400 meselesi; sistemlerin kaldırılması, devredilmesi veya tarafların kabul edeceği başka bir formülle çözülürse, F-35 konusunda çözüm büyük ölçüde kolaylaşır. Alternatif senaryolar konuşuluyor. Üçüncü bir ülkede (re-export olmadan) depolama CAATSA’nın kaldırılmasının en önemli sonucu belki de popüler konu olan F-35 olmayabilir. CAATSA kalksa Amerikan savunma sanayii bileşenlerine, motorlara, alt sistemlere, yedek parçalara ve ihracat lisanslarına erişimin kolaylaşması gibi görünmeyen faydalara odaklanmak daha mantıklı olabilir.. Türkiye’nin kendi savunma sanayii projeleri açısından bakıldığında bunlar F-35’lerden daha stratejik kalemler gibi duruyor. Kaan için de Kızılelma için de diğer envanter için de belki daha sabırlı, akışa kapılmayan bir bakış gerekiyordur.

If CAATSA is lifted, the biggest immediate benefit may not be the F-35 at all. It could be easier access to American defense components, engines, subsystems, spare parts, and export licenses. For Turkey, those may be even more important right now for its own defence industry.
